roadmap.title: Flower as reproductive organ
roadmap.summary: A typical flower carries male stamens and female pistil/carpel arranged to enable gamete formation and fertilization.

roadmap.title: Stamen and microsporangium
roadmap.summary: Anther lobes contain pollen sacs where microspore mother cells undergo meiosis to form microspores.

roadmap.title: Pollen grain
roadmap.summary: Male gametophyte with exine, intine, vegetative cell and generative cell; generative cell forms two male gametes.

roadmap.title: Pistil and ovule
roadmap.summary: The pistil has stigma, style and ovary; ovules contain nucellus, integuments, micropyle and embryo sac.

roadmap.title: Megasporogenesis
roadmap.summary: Megaspore mother cell undergoes meiosis; usually one functional megaspore forms the female gametophyte.

roadmap.title: Pollination
roadmap.summary: Transfer of pollen from anther to stigma; can be autogamy, geitonogamy or xenogamy.

roadmap.title: Pollen-pistil interaction
roadmap.summary: The stigma recognizes compatible pollen and guides pollen tube growth through the style.

roadmap.title: Double fertilization
roadmap.summary: One male gamete fuses with egg to form zygote; the other fuses with polar nuclei to form primary endosperm nucleus.

roadmap.title: Post-fertilization events
roadmap.summary: Endosperm, embryo, seed and fruit develop; ovule becomes seed and ovary becomes fruit.

roadmap.title: Apomixis and polyembryony
roadmap.summary: Some plants form seeds without fertilization or produce multiple embryos in one seed.

sections.title: Flower = reproductive laboratory
points: Stamens produce pollen grains, the male gametophytes.
The pistil/carpel houses ovules, where the female gametophyte develops.
Sepals and petals protect or attract, but the exam focus is usually stamen, pistil, pollen, ovule and embryo sac.
sections.neet: A bisexual flower has both androecium and gynoecium; unisexual flowers have either stamens or pistils.

sections.title: Anther, microsporangium and pollen formation
points: A typical angiosperm anther is bilobed and each lobe has two theca, so it is often called dithecous.
Each microsporangium has epidermis, endothecium, middle layers and tapetum around sporogenous tissue.
Microspore mother cells undergo meiosis to form microspore tetrads.
Microspores separate and mature into pollen grains.
sections.neet: Tapetum nourishes developing pollen and contributes materials for pollen wall formation.

sections.title: Pollen grain structure
points: Exine is the tough outer wall rich in sporopollenin.
Germ pores are regions where exine is absent or thin; pollen tube emerges from a germ pore.
Intine is inner and made mainly of cellulose and pectin.
At shedding, pollen may be two-celled or three-celled depending on species.
sections.neet: Sporopollenin is one of the most resistant organic materials; it helps pollen survive harsh conditions.

sections.title: Ovule and embryo sac
points: The ovule is attached to placenta by funicle; the junction region is hilum.
Integuments surround nucellus and leave a small opening called micropyle.
The mature embryo sac is usually 7-celled and 8-nucleate.
Egg apparatus has one egg cell and two synergids; antipodals lie at the chalazal end.
sections.neet: Most angiosperms show monosporic Polygonum-type embryo sac development.

sections.title: Pollination types and agents
points: Autogamy is pollen transfer within the same flower.
Geitonogamy is transfer between different flowers of the same plant; genetically similar to autogamy.
Xenogamy is transfer between flowers of different plants of the same species; it brings genetic variation.
Wind, water and animals act as pollinating agents.
sections.neet: Cleistogamous flowers never open and assure seed set even without pollinators.

sections.title: Outbreeding devices
points: Pollen release and stigma receptivity may occur at different times.
Anther and stigma may be positioned to prevent self-pollen deposition.
Self-incompatibility rejects pollen from the same plant.
Dioecy prevents both autogamy and geitonogamy.
sections.neet: Outbreeding devices discourage inbreeding and promote cross-pollination.

sections.title: Double fertilization and triple fusion
points: Pollen tube releases two male gametes into the embryo sac.
Syngamy: one male gamete + egg cell = diploid zygote.
Triple fusion: one male gamete + two polar nuclei = triploid primary endosperm nucleus.
Double fertilization is unique to flowering plants.
sections.neet: Endosperm is usually triploid because it forms from triple fusion.

sections.title: Endosperm, embryo, seed and fruit
points: Endosperm often develops before the embryo and nourishes it.
The zygote develops into embryo with radicle, plumule and cotyledons.
Ovule becomes seed; integuments become seed coats.
Ovary becomes fruit; ovary wall becomes pericarp.
sections.neet: Albuminous seeds retain endosperm; non-albuminous seeds consume it during development.

sections.title: Apomixis, parthenocarpy and polyembryony
points: Apomixis is seed formation without fertilization.
Parthenocarpy is fruit formation without fertilization, often seedless.
Polyembryony means more than one embryo occurs in a seed.
Hybrid seed industry studies apomixis because it can preserve hybrid vigour.
sections.neet: Do not confuse apomixis with parthenocarpy: apomixis concerns seed, parthenocarpy concerns fruit.

highYield: Microsporogenesis: microspore mother cell → meiosis → microspore tetrad.
Megasporogenesis: megaspore mother cell → meiosis → four megaspores; usually one functional.
Central cell contains two polar nuclei before triple fusion.
Syngamy produces zygote; triple fusion produces primary endosperm nucleus.
Double fertilization is characteristic of angiosperms.
Exine contains sporopollenin; intine contains cellulose and pectin.
Ovule → seed; ovary → fruit; integuments → seed coat.
Micropyle allows pollen tube entry and later helps water/oxygen entry into seed.
Geitonogamy is functionally cross-pollination but genetically self-pollination.
Self-incompatibility is a genetic mechanism preventing self pollen germination.
Cleistogamy assures seed set without pollinators.

quiz.question: The outermost resistant layer of pollen grain is called:
quiz.explain: Exine is the tough outer wall and contains sporopollenin.

quiz.question: A typical mature angiosperm embryo sac is:
quiz.explain: It has 7 cells but 8 nuclei: egg, two synergids, three antipodals and a central cell with two polar nuclei.

quiz.question: Triple fusion forms the:
quiz.explain: One male gamete fuses with two polar nuclei to form the primary endosperm nucleus.

quiz.question: Transfer of pollen from one flower to another flower of the same plant is:
quiz.explain: Geitonogamy occurs between different flowers of the same plant.

quiz.question: The nutritive layer of anther wall is:
quiz.explain: Tapetum provides nutrition and wall materials to developing pollen.

quiz.question: The opening left by integuments in an ovule is called:
quiz.explain: Micropyle is the small opening at one end of the ovule.

quiz.question: Which event is unique to angiosperms?
quiz.explain: Double fertilization is a defining feature of flowering plants.

quiz.question: Ovary wall develops into:
quiz.explain: After fertilization, ovary wall becomes the fruit wall or pericarp.

quiz.question: Cleistogamous flowers are important because they:
options: Never open and assure seed set
quiz.explain: They remain closed, self-pollinate and assure seed production.

flashcards.back: Formation of haploid microspores from microspore mother cells by meiosis.
Formation of haploid megaspores from the megaspore mother cell by meiosis.
Fusion of one male gamete with the egg cell to form a diploid zygote.
Fusion of the other male gamete with two polar nuclei to form primary endosperm nucleus.
Pollen transfer between flowers of different plants of the same species.
